stimulus: Many tick repellents include natural compounds that work by stimulating multiple chemosensory receptors on the ticks’ legs. As ticks develop resistance, new repellents are needed. Adesh Mehta and his team found that nootkatone, a compound extracted from Alaska yellow-cedar, can repel ticks by stimulating just one chemosensory receptor—and this receptor, Cr12, is present in every tick species known to transmit Lyme disease. Therefore, the researchers suggest that in developing new repellents, it would be most useful to ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: investigate alternative methods of extracting nootkatone from Alaska yellow-cedar.
identify additional compounds similar to nootkatone that also target Cr12 receptors.
verify the precise locations of Cr12 and other chemosensory receptors on the ticks’ legs.
determine the maximum number of distinct receptors that a single compound can stimulate.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer. The text says that nootkatone is effective because Cr12 is present in every Lyme-transmitting tick species. Because ticks may eventually develop resistance to nootkatone, the researchers’ logic supports searching for other compounds that act on the same shared receptor.

Choice A is incorrect because the text never indicates that extraction is the bottleneck. Choice C is incorrect because the text gives no hint that the location of Cr12 is unknown. Choice D is incorrect because the text’s logic does not support shifting back toward multi-receptor compounds.
